back_translation: A famine arose in the land again, in addition to the first famine that had occurred in the days of Abraham. So Isaac went to Abimelech, king of the Philistines, at the city of Gerar.
The LORD appeared to Isaac and said, "Do not go down to Egypt. Dwell in the land that I will tell you.

Dwell in this land as a foreigner, and I will be with you, and I will bless you, for I will give all these lands to you and to your offspring, and I will keep the oath that I swore to Abraham your father.
I will multiply your offspring as numerous as the stars in the sky, and I will give all these lands to your offspring, and all the nations of the earth will be blessed through your offspring,
because Abraham listened to my voice and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws."
So Isaac settled in the city of Gerar.

When the men of that place asked about his wife, he said, "She is my sister," because he was afraid to say, "She is my wife," thinking that the men of that place would kill him on account of Rebekah, since she was a beautiful woman.
When Isaac had been there many days, Abimelech king of the Philistines looked out through the window and saw Isaac caressing Rebekah his wife.
So Abimelech called Isaac and said, "See, she is certainly your wife. Then why did you say, 'She is my sister'?" Isaac answered, "Because I thought I might die on account of her."
Abimelech said, "What is this you have done to us? One of the people might easily have lain with your wife, and you would have brought guilt upon us."
So Abimelech commanded all the people, saying, "Whoever touches this man or his wife shall surely be put to death."
Isaac sowed seed in that land, and in that same year he reaped a hundredfold. And the LORD blessed him.
He grew wealthier and wealthier, until he became exceedingly rich.
He had flocks of sheep, herds of cattle, and many servants, until the Philistines envied him.
All the wells that the servants of his father had dug in the days of Abraham his father, the Philistines stopped up and filled with dirt.
Then Abimelech said to Isaac, "Go away from us, for you have become much too powerful for us."
So Isaac left that place and encamped in the Valley of Gerar and dwelt there.
Isaac re-dug the wells that had been dug in the days of Abraham his father, which the Philistines had stopped up after Abraham died, and he gave them the same names that his father had called them.
Isaac's servants dug in the valley and found there a spring well.
But the herdsmen of Gerar quarreled with Isaac's herdsmen, saying, "This water is ours." So he called the name of that well Esek, because they had contended with him.
They dug another well, and they quarreled over that one too, so he called its name Sitnah.
He moved on from there and dug another well, and no one quarreled over it, so he called its name Rehoboth, and said, "Now the LORD has opened a broad place for us, and we shall multiply in the land."
From there he went up to Beersheba.

That night the LORD appeared to him and said, "I am the God of Abraham your father. Do not be afraid, for I am with you. I will bless you and multiply your offspring for the sake of Abraham my servant."
So Isaac built an altar there and called on the name of the LORD. He pitched his tent there, and Isaac's servants dug a well there as well.
Then Abimelech came to Isaac from Gerar, with Ahuzzath his adviser and Phicol the commander of his army.
Isaac asked them, "Why have you come to me, since you hated me and drove me away from you?"
They answered, "We have clearly seen that the LORD is with you, so we thought there should be an oath between us, between us and you, and let us make a covenant with you—
that you will not harm us, just as we have not harmed you, and have done only good to you, and sent you away in peace. You are now one who is blessed by the LORD."
So Isaac prepared a feast for them, and they ate and drank together.
Early the next morning, they rose early and made an oath to one another. Isaac sent them away, and they departed in peace.
On that same day, Isaac's servants came in and told him about the well they had dug, and said to him, "We have found water."
So he called that well Shibah, and therefore the name of the city is Beersheba to this day.
When Esau was forty years old, he married Judith daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and Basemath daughter of Elon the Hittite.
They made Isaac and Rebekah grieve in spirit.
